---
title: "Health Statistics From A Geographer's Perspective"
sidebar: false
resources:
  - slides.pdf
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    page-layout: full
---

<div class="project-pdf-slides-root" hidden aria-hidden="true"></div>

:::: {.project-pdf-slides-intro}

A **bridge talk** for health-statistics audiences: what medical geography adds to their existing toolkit, with concrete methods and examples-not a full spatial-statistics course.

::::

## Project Overview

### Problem

Many **health statistics** practitioners were trained without day-to-day spatial coursework. Maps and spatial models often felt like a separate specialty rather than something they could **name, request, or interpret** in collaboration.

### Context

Health outcomes and behaviours are routinely **geographic** (aggregates, points, neighbourhood context), but cross-disciplinary vocabulary and method choice do not always line up.

### Goals

- Make **medical geography** legible: a recognizable set of tools and questions, not a vague label.
- Show **urban, suburban, and rural** examples so the value proposition is not city-only.
- Spark ideas for **applying spatial thinking** to contemporary health challenges without vendor lock-in.

### Stakeholders

- **Health statistics and allied analysts** attending the session.
- **Geography / spatial science** as the discipline supplying methods and critique.

### Final deliverable

The **slide deck (PDF)**-concept blocks, examples, and takeaways that participants can trace back to geocoding, joins, clustering, autocorrelation, and (where warranted) GWR or geographical random forests.

## Project Approach

### Scope & Assumptions

- **Scope (planned):** intro-to-intermediate methods; examples across urban, suburban, rural.
- **Not** a full methods course-a **roadmap** of what geographers add.

### Data Sources

- **Planned examples:** typical health products-**aggregates**, point events (when geocodable), **context layers**-and how they link spatially for maps and models.

### Analytical Design

- **Core (planned):** geocoding, joins, clustering, autocorrelation.
- **Stretch (planned):** GWR, geographical random forests-only where extra complexity **earns its keep**.

### Workflow / Process

**Planned pattern per block:** concept → tool → example → takeaway; intuition **before** heavy notation.

### Constraints & Considerations

- **Time** will be tight; I will avoid vendor lock-in.
- I will **name limits** up front: ecological inference, confounding, overfitting.

## Impact & Outcome

This presentation expanded awareness of how spatial methods can strengthen health statistics practice. It helped non-geographers see concrete applications for geocoding, clustering, and spatial modelling, supporting better cross-disciplinary collaboration and more geographically informed analysis.

:::: {.pdf-slides-embed-bar}
[Open PDF in new tab](slides.pdf){.btn .btn-sm .btn-outline-primary target="_blank" rel="noopener"}
::::

:::: {#pdf-slides-frame-wrap .pdf-slides-embed-full}
<iframe src="slides.pdf" title="Medical geography presentation (PDF)" class="pdf-slides-embed"></iframe>
::::
